title: The Balance Problem
concept: If the discriminator gets too good too fast, the generator gets no useful signal to learn from because the discriminator is always confidently right; if the generator gets too good too fast, the discriminator can't provide a meaningful signal either.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why is 'one loss dropping' not a reliable progress signal in GAN training?

options.text: Because the generator's and discriminator's losses are entangled — a change in one can just reflect a change in the other
Because GANs don't actually compute a loss at all
Because loss is only used for classifiers, never for GANs

exercises.explain: Right. Today: one specific way that entanglement breaks training — a balance problem that can tip in either direction.

cards.title: Both sides have to stay in the fight
cards.body: In an arm-wrestling match, training only works if both arms are still straining against each other. If one arm just goes limp, or one arm is a professional powerlifter, the match stops teaching anyone anything.

cards.title: When the discriminator wins too hard
cards.body: If the discriminator becomes an expert at spotting fakes before the generator has caught up, it starts rejecting every generated sample with near-100% confidence. That near-certain rejection doesn't just mean "the generator is bad" — it removes the useful signal the generator needs in order to improve at all.

cards.title: A poker player who reads every bluff perfectly
cards.body: Imagine a poker opponent who calls every single bluff you attempt, instantly, with total confidence. You don't just lose — you learn nothing about which parts of your bluff were weak, because the outcome ("called, every time") never changes no matter what you try. A discriminator that's too good acts the same way: its verdict is so uniformly confident that the generator gets no useful feedback on what to fix.

cards.title: The problem runs both directions
cards.body: The imbalance isn't one-sided. If the generator races ahead and starts producing images the discriminator genuinely can't tell from real ones, the discriminator's guesses become close to a coin flip — and a coin flip carries almost no useful information either. A discriminator that can't tell fakes from real can't teach the generator anything specific about how to improve further.

cards.title: "Shouldn't the 'best' discriminator always help the most?"
cards.body: It's tempting to think a highly accurate discriminator is always good news, the way a strict, accurate teacher usually helps students learn. But an adversarial discriminator's job isn't just to be accurate — it's to stay just informative enough to keep giving the generator something useful to push against. Too accurate, too early, and it stops being a useful teacher.

cards.title: Two ways to fall out of balance

left.text:
Always confident
Generator gets no signal
Gradient vanishes

right.text:
Discriminator can't tell
Guesses are near-random
Signal disappears too

exercises.prompt: Why does a discriminator that's 'too good too fast' cause problems for the generator?

options.text: It rejects every fake so confidently that the generator gets no useful gradient signal for how to improve
It starts generating its own fake images
It causes the generator to stop training entirely and crash
It makes the generator's outputs blurrier automatically

exercises.explain: Right — near-certain rejection every time removes the useful signal the generator needs.

exercises.prompt: True or false: if the generator becomes too good too fast, the discriminator can still provide a strong, useful training signal.
exercises.explain: False — the discriminator's guesses become close to random, which carries little useful signal for either network.

exercises.sentence: A discriminator that catches every fake with total confidence is like a poker opponent who reads every bluff instantly — you don't lose gracefully, you just get no useful ___ on what to fix.
exercises.explain: No matter what the generator tries, the outcome never changes — so there's no feedback to learn from.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario shows the balance problem tipping toward 'generator too strong'?

options.text: The discriminator's real-vs-fake guesses hover near 50%, barely better than a coin flip, even though it's still trying
The discriminator confidently and correctly rejects every fake image with 99% confidence
Both networks stop updating their weights at the same time

exercises.explain: Near-50% guessing means the discriminator can no longer tell real from fake — a sign the generator has pulled ahead.

exercises.prompt: Balance can break in two directions — discriminator too strong OR generator too strong. What do both breakdowns have in common?

options.text: Either way, the generator stops receiving a useful training signal, just for different reasons
Both cases cause the discriminator to be deleted from training
Both cases mean the dataset was too small
Both cases only affect image resolution

exercises.explain: Exactly. Next: why watching the loss numbers themselves doesn't reliably tell you which direction things are drifting.
